Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often spread out materials on my bed or floor and work through problems physically. I tried to dedicate about 17 hours a week, but it wasn't always structured. Sometimes I'd have intense study sessions late at night when the fear of failing kicked in, and other times I'd go days without touching anything because life just got overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how scared I was feeling about my grades. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because with 14 online courses, something was always demanding attention. Deadlines would hit and suddenly I'd be cramming, then I'd crash afterward. The stress made it hard to be consistent, and I'd often feel paralyzed about where to even start.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or panic that I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would trigger me to check what was happening. It wasn't really a positive motivation most of the time—more like dread pushing me to see what damage control I needed to do.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became truly routine for me. There were a few weeks where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart when stress got high and I started avoiding everything. The CBT sessions I had helped me recognize this avoidance pattern, but breaking it was another thing entirely. Logging in sometimes felt like facing my failures, which made it harder to do consistently.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful—I watched almost 280 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and take notes at my own pace. For a kinesthetic learner like me, being able to control the flow and physically write things down while watching made a difference. I also found the few discussion posts I made useful because explaining things to others helped cement concepts in my mind.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. With 14 courses running simultaneously, I had to triage constantly, and anything that felt optional got pushed aside. Some resources just felt too dense or theoretical, and I learn better by doing rather than reading. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate, practical application or if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. I needed to feel like I was making progress toward something concrete. Abstract discussions or theoretical readings felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics across all my courses.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. I knew they might help, but the fear of falling behind on required work made me ignore them almost entirely. Required activities had this weight of consequence that forced me to engage even when I was scared. Optional stuff just couldn't compete when I was already drowning in mandatory deadlines.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my own without being connected to the LMS. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I'd often rewrite notes by hand, create flashcards, or work through practice problems on paper. None of that showed up in any system, but it was where real learning happened for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials and study them for hours at my kitchen table, physically manipulating information to understand it. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, working through problems together on a whiteboard. The LMS would show I hadn't logged in for days, but I might have been studying intensively the whole time just not digitally.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once to download it, then never open it in the LMS again. All my actual engagement with that material happened offline where I could highlight, annotate, and physically interact with it. My login frequency probably looks low compared to how much time I actually spent with course materials.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. When my stress peaked and that scared feeling took over, I'd freeze completely. I'd look at the LMS and feel overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done, which made me want to avoid it even more. The CBT sessions helped me understand this cycle, but in the moment, the fear was paralyzing.
Q13: The workload across 14 courses was genuinely crushing, and it absolutely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd log in, feel overwhelmed, and log out without doing anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities meant I was always making impossible choices about what to prioritize. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it probably was.
Q14: Yes, many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered because I was too stressed to focus. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion forums without really reading anything, just going through the motions. The LMS might show activity, but my brain was somewhere else entirely, usually worrying about everything I wasn't getting done.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt big or scary. The irony is that avoiding them made the fear worse, which made me avoid them more. I knew assignments were important, but the anxiety about not doing them well enough would stop me from starting at all. It's a terrible cycle that the CBT sessions helped me see more clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could break through my avoidance. I'd have intense bursts of activity right before something was due, which probably shows in my data as sporadic engagement. Without a deadline looming, it was too easy to tell myself I'd do it tomorrow. The fear of the deadline eventually outweighed the fear of engaging.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more, but critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a while. With my low resilience, negative feedback hit hard. I tried to use it constructively, but emotionally it was difficult not to take it personally.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across 14 courses. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller, manageable chunks, but often the structure assumed you were keeping up consistently, which I wasn't. When I fell behind, the structure made it hard to catch up because everything built on previous material I'd missed.
Q19: Clear deadlines and organized modules made engagement easier because I knew exactly what was expected. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many different places to check for information. Also, courses that were heavily reading-based were harder for me as a kinesthetic learner—I needed more interactive elements.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around, watching videos without absorbing anything, but being too scared or overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just circling the drain, trying to engage but not quite managing it.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My attendance in the LMS was only about 45%, but I passed the course because a lot of my learning happened offline. I'd download materials and study them extensively away from the platform. My exam score and final grade don't fully reflect how little I appeared to engage online because the real work was happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or avoidance patterns. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional state, quality of engagement, and whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture fear, motivation, or the struggle behind each login.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and thinking about course concepts even outside of study time. For me, real engagement is when I can apply what I'm learning to something tangible, when it clicks and becomes part of how I understand the world, not just information I'm memorizing to pass.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I've been throughout this course, or how hard I've worked to manage that fear through CBT. It can't show the hours I spent studying offline, the peer study sessions, or the internal battle every time I had to decide whether to log in or avoid everything. My emotional journey and personal growth are completely invisible to the data.
